storyboard_metadata.theme: Cloud Computing Fundamentals — From Ground to Sky
storyboard_metadata.style: Clean modern motion graphics with flat design icons, smooth transitions, and layered reveals

scenes.narration_text: What if you could access unlimited computing power — without owning a single server? Welcome to Cloud Computing.
scenes.animation_description: Opens on a dark screen. A single glowing cursor blinks. Text types out 'What if...' letter by letter. Then a massive wall of server racks EXPANDS outward from the center, filling the frame. The servers dissolve into particles that float upward and reform into a soft glowing cloud shape. The title 'Cloud Computing' PULSES into existence beneath the cloud with a subtle lens flare.
scenes.visual_metaphor: Servers transforming into a cloud — physical hardware dissolving into an abstract, accessible resource
scenes.transition_to_next: The cloud shape gently drifts upward and the background lightens into a sky gradient, pulling us into the next scene

scenes.narration_text: Cloud computing is the on-demand delivery of computing resources — servers, storage, databases, and software — over the internet, with pay-as-you-go pricing. Think of it like electricity: you plug in and use what you need, without building your own power plant.
scenes.animation_description: A laptop icon sits at screen center. From the laptop, a dotted line FLOWS upward into a large cloud icon. Inside the cloud, small icons (server, database cylinder, gear, code brackets) fade in one by one, orbiting gently. Then a split comparison BUILDS: on the left, a person cranking a generator (labeled 'Own Infrastructure'); on the right, a person plugging into a wall socket (labeled 'Cloud'). The wall socket side glows green. A stat counter rolls up: '$600B+ market in 2023 — 94% of enterprises use cloud.'
scenes.visual_metaphor: Electricity utility analogy — plug in to computing power instead of running your own generator
scenes.transition_to_next: The cloud icon shrinks and slides to the right, becoming the endpoint of a horizontal timeline that EXPANDS leftward into history

scenes.scene_title: The Evolution — From Mainframes to Cloud
scenes.narration_text: Computing has evolved through four major eras. The 1960s mainframe — one giant machine, many users. The 1980s PC revolution — one machine per person. The 1990s client-server model — connected networks, but expensive infrastructure. Then in 2006, Amazon launched AWS, and the cloud era began. Computing shifted from a capital expense to an operational one — like switching from buying a car to using a ride-share.
scenes.animation_description: A horizontal timeline BUILDS from left to right across the screen. Era 1 (1960s): a large mainframe icon EXPANDS with tiny terminal icons connecting to it via lines. Era 2 (1980s): the mainframe shrinks, and many small PC icons POP in across the screen, isolated from each other. Era 3 (1990s): PCs CONNECT with network lines to a central server rack, a building icon appears labeled 'Data Center'. Era 4 (2006): the server rack and building DISSOLVE upward into a cloud, the AWS logo fades in briefly. A car icon morphs into a ride-share icon. Each era lights up in sequence with a subtle PULSE.
scenes.visual_metaphor: Timeline of computing eras, ending with physical infrastructure dissolving into the cloud; car-to-rideshare transition for CapEx-to-OpEx shift
scenes.transition_to_next: The timeline compresses into a thin line that becomes a divider splitting the screen into two halves for the comparison scene

scenes.scene_title: Traditional vs. Cloud — The Great Comparison
scenes.narration_text: Traditional computing means owning everything — servers, cooling, staff, and the million-dollar data center. Cloud computing means renting what you need, scaling in minutes, and paying only for what you use. It is the difference between owning a private library and having a card to the world's largest one.
scenes.animation_description: Split screen with a vertical divider. LEFT side (Traditional): a building icon EXPANDS, server racks fill a room, a technician icon appears, dollar signs stack up (labeled 'CapEx: $$$'), a clock shows '12-18 months to deploy', a small fixed bar graph shows limited scale. RIGHT side (Cloud): a laptop icon connects to a cloud, auto-scaling arrows PULSE up and down, a meter spins (labeled 'OpEx: pay-per-use'), a stopwatch shows 'Deploy in minutes', a bar graph dynamically GROWS tall. The divider then slides left, squishing the traditional side as the cloud side EXPANDS to fill the screen. Text overlay: 'Cloud reduces IT costs by 30-50%.'
scenes.visual_metaphor: Split-screen battle — cramped server room vs. open cloud sky; private library vs. global library card
scenes.transition_to_next: The cloud side zooms in, and the cloud icon opens up like a cross-section, revealing internal layers for the architecture scene

scenes.narration_text: Cloud architecture has distinct layers. At the top, the front-end — your browser, your app. At the bottom, the back-end — massive data centers with thousands of servers. Connecting them, the network — the internet itself. In between, virtualization technology slices one physical machine into many virtual ones. And orchestrating it all, the management layer — handling scaling, security, and billing.
scenes.animation_description: A layered cake diagram BUILDS from bottom to top. Layer 1 (Physical Infrastructure): server rack icons and storage cylinders slide in from the bottom, labeled 'Servers, Storage, Networking'. Layer 2 (Virtualization): a translucent overlay EXPANDS over the hardware, showing one server splitting into multiple VM icons with a gentle PULSE. Layer 3 (Management): gear icons, a shield, and a billing meter FLOW in from the sides, orbiting the stack. Layer 4 (Network): glowing connection lines PULSE between the layers and extend upward to the internet cloud icon. Layer 5 (Front-End): a laptop, phone, and tablet icon descend from the top and CONNECT to the network lines. The whole structure gently PULSES like a living system. Side note text: 'One server can run 10-20+ virtual machines.'
scenes.visual_metaphor: Layered cake / hotel analogy — guests see rooms (front-end), but behind the scenes are plumbing and staff (back-end) connected by hallways (network)
scenes.transition_to_next: The layered diagram compresses into a compact icon, and five nodes EXPAND outward from it in a pentagon formation

scenes.narration_text: NIST defines five essential characteristics of cloud computing. One: On-Demand Self-Service — provision resources instantly, like a vending machine. Two: Broad Network Access — reach your cloud from any device, anywhere. Three: Resource Pooling — shared infrastructure serving many users efficiently. Four: Rapid Elasticity — scale up and down like a rubber band stretching with demand. Five: Measured Service — pay only for what you consume, metered like electricity.
scenes.animation_description: Five nodes are arranged in a pentagon. Each node PULSES and EXPANDS as it is introduced. Node 1 (On-Demand): a vending machine icon drops a server into a hand. Node 2 (Broad Access): a radio tower icon broadcasts signal rings outward to a laptop, phone, and tablet. Node 3 (Resource Pooling): a pool icon with multiple user silhouettes diving in, resources flowing between them. Node 4 (Elasticity): a rubber band stretches vertically as a traffic graph spikes, then contracts as traffic drops — server count follows in sync. Node 5 (Measured): an electricity meter spins, a receipt prints showing line items. After all five appear, glowing lines CONNECT them all into the pentagon, and the whole shape PULSES once in unison. Stat overlay: 'Auto-scaling adds instances in 30-60 seconds.'
scenes.visual_metaphor: Five pillars forming a connected pentagon — vending machine, radio tower, shared pool, rubber band, electricity meter
scenes.transition_to_next: The pentagon shrinks to center screen, and four quadrants EXPAND outward from it, each representing a deployment model

scenes.scene_title: Deployment Models — How Clouds Are Organized
scenes.narration_text: Clouds come in four flavors. Public Cloud — like a public bus: shared, affordable, run by providers like AWS, Azure, and GCP. Private Cloud — like your own car: full control, higher cost, used by banks and governments. Hybrid Cloud — drive your car daily, take the bus for long trips: the most popular enterprise model. Community Cloud — a shared office for organizations with common needs. Over 80% of enterprises now use a hybrid or multi-cloud strategy.
scenes.animation_description: Four quadrants EXPAND from center. TOP-LEFT (Public): an open globe icon with the AWS/Azure/GCP logos orbiting it, a bus icon slides across. TOP-RIGHT (Private): a locked building icon with a key, a car icon parks beside it. BOTTOM-LEFT (Hybrid): the screen splits diagonally — half bus, half car — then they merge into a combined icon with a bridge connecting a building to a cloud. BOTTOM-RIGHT (Community): multiple building icons cluster together inside a shared fence boundary, a handshake icon PULSES above them. A pie chart BUILDS in the center showing market share: AWS 31%, Azure 25%, GCP 11%, Others. The hybrid quadrant glows brightest with an '80% adoption' badge.

color_palette.accent: #7C3AED (purple for private) / #F59E0B (amber for hybrid) / #06B6D4 (cyan for community)

scenes.visual_metaphor: Transportation analogy — bus (public), car (private), car+bus (hybrid), carpool (community)
scenes.transition_to_next: The four quadrants stack vertically and morph into a responsibility chart with three columns, transitioning into service models

scenes.narration_text: Cloud services come in three layers of abstraction. IaaS — Infrastructure as a Service: you get raw building blocks — virtual machines, storage, networking. You manage the operating system and everything above it. Like renting an empty plot with utilities. PaaS — Platform as a Service: the platform is ready — just bring your code. Like a furnished office. SaaS — Software as a Service: everything is done for you — just log in and use it. Like ordering delivery pizza. The more abstraction, the less control but more convenience.
scenes.animation_description: Three tall columns BUILD side by side, each made of stacked layers (Networking, Storage, Servers, Virtualization, OS, Middleware, Runtime, Data, Applications). COLUMN 1 (IaaS): bottom four layers fill with blue (provider-managed), top five layers fill with orange (user-managed). A Lego brick icon sits atop. COLUMN 2 (PaaS): bottom seven layers fill blue, top two fill orange. A workbench icon sits atop. COLUMN 3 (SaaS): all layers fill blue except a tiny user icon at the top. A pizza delivery box icon sits atop. Then the pizza analogy EXPANDS below: four panels slide in from left to right — 'Make at home' (full kitchen), 'Buy ingredients' (IaaS), 'Buy pre-made and bake' (PaaS), 'Order delivery' (SaaS). Each panel has fewer tasks highlighted. Labels show examples: EC2, Heroku/Vercel, Gmail/Slack.
scenes.visual_metaphor: Stacked responsibility chart — blue layers (provider) vs. orange layers (user); pizza analogy from homemade to delivery
scenes.transition_to_next: The columns compress and a balance scale EXPANDS from the center, one side rising, the other falling

scenes.narration_text: Cloud computing brings powerful advantages: cost savings of 20 to 30 percent, instant scalability, global access, built-in disaster recovery, and faster deployment. But there are real limitations too: internet dependency, security concerns, vendor lock-in, limited control, compliance challenges, and the risk of hidden costs. The key is understanding both sides to make informed decisions.
scenes.animation_description: A large balance scale sits at center. LEFT SIDE (Advantages): green upward arrows FLY in one at a time — each labeled (Cost, Scale, Access, Recovery, Speed, Auto-Updates, Green). The left pan rises as benefits stack up. RIGHT SIDE (Limitations): orange warning triangles slide in — each labeled (Internet, Security, Lock-In, Control, Compliance, Hidden Costs). The right pan rises slightly but the scale tips decisively toward advantages. Green checkmarks PULSE next to each advantage, orange caution icons next to each limitation. Stat overlay: '20-30% average cost savings.' A road metaphor appears briefly: a highway with green signs (advantages) and speed bumps (limitations).
scenes.visual_metaphor: Balance scale tipping toward advantages; highway with green signs and speed bumps
scenes.transition_to_next: The scale fades and a globe EXPANDS from center, with cloud provider logos orbiting it

scenes.narration_text: The cloud landscape is led by three giants. AWS — the pioneer, with over 200 services and 31% market share. Microsoft Azure — the enterprise favorite at 25%. Google Cloud — the data and AI powerhouse at 11%. And platforms like Vercel are making cloud deployment as simple as a single Git push. Together, they power the digital world.
scenes.animation_description: A globe rotates slowly at center. The AWS logo FLIES in from the left and orbits the globe, leaving an orange trail — a pie slice expands showing 31%. The Azure logo FLIES in from the right with a blue trail — 25% slice. The GCP logo rises from below with a red/yellow/green/blue trail — 11% slice. The remaining slice fills with smaller logos (Alibaba, IBM, DigitalOcean, Heroku). Then the Vercel logo PULSES in prominently with a special callout: 'Deploy in under 30 seconds.' Data center location dots PULSE across the globe (30+ regions). The logos settle into a clean grid layout with one-line descriptions fading in beneath each.
scenes.visual_metaphor: Logos orbiting a globe like satellites — each provider illuminating regions of the world they serve
scenes.transition_to_next: The globe and logos shrink into a laptop screen, and a code editor EXPANDS showing real deployment steps

scenes.scene_title: Theory Meets Practice — Cloud Deployment in Action
scenes.narration_text: Now theory becomes practice. Watch: code on a laptop, a single Git push, and within seconds the cloud builds, tests, and deploys your application — live, with a URL anyone in the world can access. That is the power of cloud computing in action.
scenes.animation_description: A laptop screen EXPANDS to fill the frame, showing a minimal code editor with a few lines of code. A terminal appears at the bottom. The command 'git push' types out and executes. A chain reaction FLOWS rightward: Code icon -> arrow -> Git icon -> arrow -> Cloud Platform icon (build gears spinning) -> arrow -> Globe icon with a live URL. Each step PULSES as the chain progresses. The URL 'myapp.vercel.app' appears and a browser window EXPANDS showing a live website. Small device icons (phone, tablet, desktop) around the world CONNECT to the site with glowing lines. A 'Deploy in 30 seconds' timer counts down in the corner. Confetti particles burst subtly when deployment completes.
scenes.visual_metaphor: Chain reaction from code to live site — the deploy button as a trigger for automated magic
scenes.transition_to_next: The browser window with the live site zooms out and floats upward into a cloud, joining all previous concepts in the closing scene

scenes.scene_title: The Big Picture — Cloud Is the Future
scenes.narration_text: Cloud computing has transformed how we build, deploy, and scale technology. From a $600 billion industry to the backbone of modern innovation — the cloud is not just the future. It is the present. Start building.
scenes.animation_description: All key icons from previous scenes FLOW inward from the edges and assemble into a grand unified cloud composition at center: servers, VMs, the five NIST nodes, deployment models, service model layers, provider logos, and the deployed website — all orbiting gently inside a large translucent cloud shape. The cloud PULSES with energy. The stat '$600B+ and growing' types out below. Then everything gracefully FADES except the cloud shape and the final text: 'Cloud Computing — Start Building.' The cloud glows warmly and a cursor blinks at the end of 'Start Building', inviting action. Credits fade in: 'Introduction to Cloud Computing — SVIT College — Abhinav Gupta.'
scenes.visual_metaphor: All concepts converging into one unified cloud — the sum of all parts forming a living ecosystem
scenes.transition_to_next: Final fade to black with a gentle glow
